bee_quiz.question: The pushing force that air puts on objects all around us is called what?
The blanket of air that surrounds Earth and lets us breathe is called the what?
Air squeezed tightly inside a bicycle tire is an example of air being what?
In the cup-in-water trick, the paper towel inside the cup stays dry because air did what?

choices.d: took up space and blocked water from entering

bee_quiz.question: To prove air has weight using two balloons, scientists in the chapter used a what?
A parachute slowing your fall is an example of which property of air?
Some people think air is just empty space. According to the chapter, air is actually what?

choices.a: only a feeling on your skin
choices.b: a real substance with weight and that takes up space

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Air feels like nothing, so kids assume it's empty space — but it has weight, takes space, and pushes on things.

bee_quiz.question: A drink rises through a straw not because you 'pulled' it but because of what?

choices.c: air pressure outside the cup pushing the drink up

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Kids think sucking pulls the drink — actually outside air pressure pushes the drink up because you removed the air inside the straw.

bee_quiz.question: A parachute slows a falling person not because the parachute is heavy but because of what?

choices.a: air pushing back against the parachute

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Kids assume a parachute helps because of its weight — but the real cause is air pushing back on the wide surface.

bee_quiz.question: In a hot-air balloon the order of events is: the air is heated → the air does what → the balloon rises?
With a parachute on, you fall slowly. Without one, you fall fast. Falling SLOWER happens in which case?

choices.b: if the parachute is heavier than the person
choices.c: both cases fall at the same speed

bee_quiz.question: On the two-balloon balance, the side that sinks DOWN toward the ground is the side that is what?
Which of these is **NOT** a property of air, according to the chapter?

choices.b: air can be seen with your eyes normally

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is **NOT** something the chapter says air does?
Which of these is **NOT** a place where you would normally find air?

choices.c: in outer space far from Earth

word_cards.definition: The mix of invisible gases all around us that we breathe — it has weight and takes up space.
word_cards.example: Blow into a balloon and the balloon swells — that's the same gases you're breathing right now.

word_cards.definition: The blanket of breathable gases that wraps around our planet Earth.
word_cards.example: Astronauts leave this blanket when they fly to space, which is why their suits carry oxygen.

word_cards.definition: The force that the invisible gases all around us put on every surface they touch.
word_cards.example: Sucking on a straw lets the outside push do the real work — your drink rises because of this.

word_cards.definition: Squeezed into a smaller space than it normally takes up.
word_cards.example: The hard rubber on a bicycle tire is full of squeezed-tight gas — that's what makes it bouncy.

word_cards.definition: A real thing in the world that has weight and takes up space.
word_cards.example: Water, rock, and air are each one of these — they are all real, not nothing.

word_cards.definition: The force that pushes back against a moving object and slows it down as it travels through it.
word_cards.example: A parachute opens wide so MORE pushing-back happens, and the person falls slowly.

word_cards.definition: A space with no air (or any other gas) in it at all.
word_cards.example: Outer space, far from any planet, is mostly this — sound cannot travel there because sound needs air to carry it.

word_cards.definition: A simple tool that compares the weight of two things by hanging them on either side of a stick.
word_cards.example: Tie a full balloon and a popped balloon to a stick — the full side hangs lower because it's heavier.

word_cards.definition: How heavy something is — the pull of Earth's gravity on it.
word_cards.example: A full bag of air is a tiny bit heavier than the same bag empty.

word_cards.definition: Air that is moving through the world from one place to another.
word_cards.example: On a stormy day you can FEEL the air pushing on your jacket — that's the same thing happening.

v2_changes: Q3: rephrased with example-to-category framing (bicycle tire example)
Q11: removed colour-based distractor; replaced with weight-comparison concept
Air pressure card: definition rewritten per Gemini's exact suggestion
Air resistance card: definition rewritten to remove 'gases/air'
_self_check.instrument_or_unit_reason: Chapter explicitly uses a 'balance' to weigh balloons. Q5 tests the instrument name.
